elements.sent-2411-001: We propose the first Bayesian methods for detecting change points in high-dimensional mean and covariance structures.
elements.sent-2411-002: These methods are constructed using pairwise Bayes factors, leveraging modularization to identify significant changes in individual components efficiently.
elements.sent-2411-003: We establish that the proposed methods consistently detect and estimate change points under much milder conditions than existing approaches in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-004: Additionally, we demonstrate that their localization rates are nearly optimal in terms of rates.
elements.sent-2411-005: The practical performance of the proposed methods is evaluated through extensive simulation studies, where they are compared to state-of-the-art techniques.
elements.sent-2411-006: The results show comparable or superior performance across most scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-007: Notably, the methods effectively detect change points whenever signals of sufficient magnitude are present, irrespective of the number of signals.
elements.sent-2411-008: Finally, we apply the proposed methods to genetic and financial datasets, illustrating their practical utility in real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-009: Key words: High-dimensional change point detection; mean vector; covariance matrix; maximum pairwise Bayes factor.
elements.sent-2411-010: Detecting change points in the mean vector or covariance matrix of multivariate data can be crucial in various scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-011: Changes in the mean vector may indicate shifts in the central tendency of the data, while changes in the covariance matrix signify alterations in the relationships among variables.
elements.sent-2411-012: For instance, in financial analysis, representing the returns of various assets as a multivariate time series allows the detection of changes in the mean or covariance during significant events or financial crises.
elements.sent-2411-013: Similarly, in manufacturing, monitoring multivariate data related to product characteristics helps ensure product quality.
elements.sent-2411-014: Identifying change points in the mean vector or covariance matrix can signal deviations in production processes, enabling timely interventions to maintain quality and minimize defects.
elements.sent-2411-015: Suppose that we observe a sequence of random vectors $X_1, \ldots , X_n$ independently from $p$-dimensional Gaussian distributions, say $X_i \overset{ind.}
elements.sent-2411-016: We say that there exist $K$ change points, $\{i_1,\ldots, i_K\}$, if $1< i_1 < \cdots < i_K < n$ and \begin{equation}( \mu_{i_k }, \sg_{i_k }) = \cdots = (\mu_{i_{k+1}-1} ,\sg_{i_{k+1}-1}) , \quad k=0,\ldots, K , (\mu_{i_k}, \sg_{i_k}) \neq (\mu_{i_{k+1}}, \sg_{i_{k+1}}) , \quad k=1,\ldots, K,\end{equation} where $i_0=1$ and $i_{K+1 =n+1$.}
elements.sent-2411-017: In general, the number of change points $K$ and their locations are unknown.
elements.sent-2411-018: We focus on scenarios where changes occur in either the mean vector or the covariance matrix.
elements.sent-2411-019: Our primary objectives are (i) testing for the existence of change points and (ii) estimating the number and locations of change points if they are present.
elements.sent-2411-020: Notably, we consider a high-dimensional setting where the number of variables $p$ increases with the sample size $n$ and may even be much larger than $n$.
elements.sent-2411-021: In this paper, we develop two Bayesian methods for detecting change points in in mean and covariance structures, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-022: These methods build on the maximum pairwise Bayes factor approach introduced by \cite{lee2021maximum and \cite{lee2024bayesian , adapting it to address the challenges of high-dimensional change point detection.}}
elements.sent-2411-023: Specifically, we select a window size $n_w > 1$ and compute the maximum pairwise Bayes factors using only the $n_w$ points before and after each central point $l$.
elements.sent-2411-024: Choosing an appropriate window size $n_w$ is crucial, and the optimal choices depends on the data characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-025: To address this, we propose multiscale methods that consider multiple window sizes simultaneously, thereby mitigating the impact of suboptimal window size selection and achieving stable performance.
elements.sent-2411-026: Furthermore, we extend our methods to handle scenarios where both mean and covariance changes occur simultaneously, providing a comprehensive solution for change point detection in complex data structures.
elements.sent-2411-027: It is worth noting that while the pairwise Bayes factor approach has been previously applied to two-sample hypothesis testing, as in \cite{lee2021maximum and \cite{lee2024bayesian , extending such an approach to high-dimensional change point detection poses significant challenges, particularly on the theoretical front.}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Our work introduces technical innovations to establish minimax-optimal localization rates, making this the first Bayesian method to achieve such results in high-dimensional change point detection.
elements.sent-2411-029: While several frequentist procedures have been proposed for change point detection in high-dimensional settings (e.g., \cite{dette2022estimating , \cite{wang2021optimal , \cite{grundy2020high , \cite{wang2018high , \cite{avanesov2018change , and \cite{matteson2014nonparametric ), to the best of our knowledge, no Bayesian methods have been developed to address high-dimensional change point detections.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-030: The Bayesian methods proposed in this paper offer significant advantages, requiring weaker conditions compared to existing frequentist approaches—details of which will be elaborated later.
elements.sent-2411-031: Moreover, Bayesian change point detection inherently differs from its frequentist counterparts, with unique characteristics, making the development of such methods both important and of independent interest.
elements.sent-2411-032: The main contributions of this paper are threefold.
elements.sent-2411-033: Firstly, we propose the first Bayesian tests for change point detection in either the mean or the covariance structure in high-dimensional settings, supported by theoretical foundations.
elements.sent-2411-034: We establish the consistency of the proposed Bayesian tests under both null and alternative hypotheses with mild conditions (Theorems \ref{thm:mean_changepoint and \ref{thm:cov_changepoint ).}}
elements.sent-2411-035: Secondly, building on the proposed Bayesian tests, we develop Bayesian change-point detection methods that estimate the location and number of change points, which consistently estimate both the number and locations of change points (Theorems \ref{thm:changepoint_est_mean and \ref{thm:changepoint_est ).}}
elements.sent-2411-036: The proposed methods require weaker assumptions than existing approaches, while demonstrating nearly minimax localization rates (Theorems \ref{thm:changepoint_est_mean_lb and \ref{thm:changepoint_est_lowerbound ).}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Thirdly, the proposed Bayesian tests and change point detection methods are scalable to high-dimensional settings and feature straightforward implementation.
elements.sent-2411-038: Leveraging the modularization framework from \cite{lee2021maximum and \cite{lee2024bayesian , these methods significantly enhance computational efficiency by avoiding the inversion of singular matrices, making them practical and efficient for real-world applications.}}
elements.sent-2411-039: The remaining of the paper are structured as follows: Section \ref{sec:change_mean (Section \ref{sec:change_cov ) introduces a Bayesian test for detecting change points and estimation methods for estimating the locations of these points in the mean (covariance) structure.}}
elements.sent-2411-040: Sections \ref{sec:simulation and \ref{sec:real assess the practical performance of the proposed methods through simulation studies and real data analysis, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-041: Concluding remarks are provided in Section \ref{sec:disc , and proofs of the main results can be found in the supplementary material.}

elements.sent-2411-209: In this section, we compare the proposed change point detection methods with several state-of-the-art methods.
elements.sent-2411-210: The comparison spans a wide range of scenarios, including both single and multiple change points, varying dimensions, different numbers and magnitudes of signals, and various covariance matrix structures.
elements.sent-2411-211: We consider situations where changes occur either in the mean or in the covariance, but not both.
elements.sent-2411-212: As competing methods, we choose the change point detection methods proposed by \cite{dette2022estimating , \cite{grundy2020high , \cite{wang2018high and \cite{matteson2014nonparametric .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-213: For simplicity, we refer to these competing methods as Dette, Geomcp, Inspect and Edivisive, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-214: Dette is designed to detect a single change in the covariance structure and will be included only in relevant scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-215: Inspect is intended for detecting changes in the mean structure and will be compared only in applicable settings.
elements.sent-2411-216: Geomcp can detect change points in both the mean and diagonal covariance structures.
elements.sent-2411-217: Edivisive is a nonparametric change point detection method.
elements.sent-2411-218: Selecting an appropriate window size is critical for the performance of the proposed methods.
elements.sent-2411-219: However, the optimal window size is case-dependent, which makes it challenging to choose the right size when there is limited prior knowledge about the data.
elements.sent-2411-220: To alleviate this issue and improve stability across various scenarios, we propose a multiscale approach inspired by \cite{avanesov2018change .}
elements.sent-2411-221: Let \(\mathcal{N = \{n_w^{(r) : r=1\ldots, |\mathcal{N | \} \) denote the set of window sizes, where $n_w^{(1) < \cdots < n_w^{(|\mathcal{N |)}$ and $|\mathcal{N |$ is an odd number.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-222: For $r = 1, \ldots, |\mathcal{N |$, let \( \mathcal{I _r = \{\widehat{i _k^{(r) : k = 1, \ldots, \widehat{K _r\} \) denote the set of detected change points from the mxPBF with window size \( n_w^{(r) \).}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-223: We sequentially use the window sizes $n_w^{(r)}$ to group the change points obtained from each mxPBF.
elements.sent-2411-224: Specifically, for each \( \widehat{i _k^{(1) \in \mathcal{I _1 \), we first construct intervals based on the narrowest window $n_w^{(1)}$, defined as \( [\widehat{i _k^{(1) - n_{w ^{(1) + 1, \widehat{i _k^{(1) + n_{w ^{(1) - 1] \).}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-225: We then count how many points are included in each interval among the estimated change points $\{\mathcal{I _r : r = 1, \ldots, |\mathcal{N | \}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-226: If an interval contains at least $(|\mathcal{N |+1)/2$ points, the change points within that interval are grouped together.}
elements.sent-2411-227: Once no interval contains $(|\mathcal{N |+1)/2$ or more points, we proceed to the next window size, $n_w^{(2)}$, and use the change points in $\mathcal{I _2$ to construct the intervals $ [\widehat{i _k^{(2) - n_w^{(2) + 1, \widehat{i _k^{(2) + n_w^{(2) - 1] $.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-228: From these intervals, we again group change points if at least $(|\mathcal{N |+1)/2$ change points, excluding those that have already been grouped, fall within a single interval.}
elements.sent-2411-229: If overlapping intervals contain exactly the same number of points, we prioritize grouping the interval with the smaller sample variance of the points within it.
elements.sent-2411-230: This process is repeated until groups are identified using the final window size $n_w^{(|\mathcal{N |)}$.}
elements.sent-2411-231: Each of the resulting groups corresponds to a single change point, and the final estimate of the change point is computed as the average of $\widehat{i _k^{(r)}$ within each group.}
elements.sent-2411-232: Since the final decision is based on a majority rule, we refer to this multiscale approach as $\text{mxPBF\_major}$ throughout.
elements.sent-2411-233: In the numerical studies, we found that $\text{mxPBF\_major}$ achieves reasonable performance across a variety of scenarios, as detailed in the supplementary material.
elements.sent-2411-234: This multiscale method can also be theoretically justified.

elements.sent-2411-340: In this section, we apply the proposed change point detection methods to real datasets.
elements.sent-2411-341: In practice, it is unclear whether changes occur in the mean, covariance or both.
elements.sent-2411-342: To address this issue, we apply both of the proposed change point detection methods sequentially.
elements.sent-2411-343: We first apply the method for detecting change points in the covariance structure.
elements.sent-2411-344: To satisfy the assumption that the mean is a zero vector, we roughly center the observations using a given window size $n_w$.
elements.sent-2411-345: Specifically, given \(X_{ij}\) where \(1 \leq i \leq n\) and \(1 \leq j \leq p\), and a window size \(n_w \in \mathcal{N}\), the centered data is defined as \(\tilde{X _{ij = X_{ij - \bar{X _{\max(1, i - \lfloor n_w/2 \rfloor):\min(i + \lfloor n_w/2 \rfloor, n), j}\), where \(\bar{X _{a:b, j = (b-a+1)^{-1 \sum_{k=a ^b X_{kj}\).}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-346: Then we apply the change point detection method for covariance matrices.
elements.sent-2411-347: If no changes are detected, we then apply the proposed change point detection method for mean vectors to the original, uncentered dataset.
elements.sent-2411-348: If one or more change points are detected, we divide the data based on the estimated change points.
elements.sent-2411-349: Since each segment is expected to share a common covariance matrix, we apply the proposed change point detection method for mean vectors within each segment.
elements.sent-2411-350: In this case, if some segments are smaller than twice the largest window size we used, we only use the available windows.
elements.sent-2411-351: Finally, we combine the change points detected in both the mean and covariance structures.
elements.sent-2411-352: Note that this process prioritizes changes in covariance matrices over changes in mean vectors.
elements.sent-2411-353: We first examine the comparative genomic hybridization microarray dataset from \cite{bleakley2011group , which examines DNA copy number variations in individuals with bladder tumors.}
elements.sent-2411-354: This technique involves comparing the fluorescence intensity levels of DNA fragments between a test sample and a normal reference sample to detect chromosomal copy number abnormalities.
elements.sent-2411-355: The dataset consists of log-intensity ratio measurements from 43 individuals $(p=43)$ with bladder tumors, collected at 2,215 positions $(n=2215)$ across the genome.
elements.sent-2411-356: This dataset is available in the R package ecp \citep{matteson2014nonparametric .}
elements.sent-2411-357: Following the method described in \cite{grundy2020high , we scale each series using the median absolute deviation prior to applying the detection methods.}
elements.sent-2411-358: For Geomcp, we use the CROPS algorithm \citep{haynes2017computationally as suggested in \cite{grundy2020high .}}
elements.sent-2411-359: For the other parameters, we use the same settings as described before.
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width = \textwidth]{Plots/real_gene.png} \caption{Log-intensity ratio measurements from microarray data of 43 individuals, with vertical dotted lines marking the identified change points.} \label{fig:realgenom}
elements.sent-2411-360: As illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:realgenom , mxPBF\_major identifies 64 change points, while Inspect and Edivisive detect 59 and 64, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-361: There is a strong overlap among the change points detected by these methods.
elements.sent-2411-362: Within a margin of 15 points, approximately 83\% of the change points identified by mxPBF\_major match those detected by Inspect, and 86\% overlap with those detected by Edivisive.
elements.sent-2411-363: Similarly, about 80\% of the change points detected by Inspect and 88\% of those detected by Edivisive correspond to those identified by mxPBF\_major.
elements.sent-2411-364: Meanwhile, Geomcp detects 48 change points when the number of changes in distance and angle mappings are set to 46 and 42, respectively, based on diagnostic plots.
elements.sent-2411-365: The relatively small number of change points detected by Geomcp can be attributed to its design, which identifies only those changes that appear common across multiple individuals \citep{grundy2020high .}
elements.sent-2411-366: Approximately 85\% of the change points detected by Geomcp align with those identified by mxPBF\_major, suggesting that mxPBF\_major is also capable of detecting these changes.
elements.sent-2411-367: Thus, mxPBF\_major effectively detects not only individually specific change points but also those common across multiple individuals.
elements.sent-2411-368: We now consider the daily log returns of the closing stock prices for companies in the S\&P 500 from January 2015 to December 2016.
elements.sent-2411-369: The dataset was loaded using the R package SP500R \citep{foretsp500r .}
elements.sent-2411-370: The initial dataset is a matrix with \(n = 380\) rows, corresponding to dates, and 500 columns representing companies.
elements.sent-2411-371: First, we remove 21 columns containing missing values, which results in \(p = 479\).
elements.sent-2411-372: We then scale each series using the median absolute deviation, following the approach outlined in \cite{grundy2020high .}
elements.sent-2411-373: For Geomcp, we use the normal cost function and the CROPS algorithm \citep{haynes2017computationally , as suggested in \cite{grundy2020high .}}
elements.sent-2411-374: For the other parameters, we use the same settings as described previously.
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width = \textwidth]{Plots/real_stock.png} \caption{Log returns of 479 companies within the S\&P 500 are displayed, with vertical dotted lines indicating the identified change points.} \label{fig:realstock}
elements.sent-2411-375: As shown in the figure \ref{fig:realstock , mxPBF\_major identifies 9 change points.}
elements.sent-2411-376: These detected change points correspond to events that broadly impacted many companies.
elements.sent-2411-377: For example, the change points in August 2015 and January 2016 align with significant declines in the Chinese stock markets, which affected global markets, including the S\&P 500 index.
elements.sent-2411-378: The changepoint in early July 2016 likely corresponds to the announcement and outcome of the British referendum to leave the European Union, while the one in late October reflects the U.S.. presidential election.
elements.sent-2411-379: Inspect and Edivisive methods detect 11 and 10 change points, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-380: For Geomcp, we select 13 and 5 as the number of changes in distance and angle mappings, respectively, resulting in a total of 8 final change points.
elements.sent-2411-381: Similar to the result in \eqref{subsec:realgenomic , approximately 88\% change points detected by Geomcp align with those identified by mxPBF\_major within 15 time points.}
elements.sent-2411-382: Furthermore, mxPBF\_major and Edivisive demonstrate a strong relationship, as all change points detected by both methods overlap.
elements.sent-2411-383: In this paper, we proposed Bayesian change point detection methods for high-dimensional mean and covariance structures, building upon the mxPBF framework \citep{lee2024bayesian .}
elements.sent-2411-384: We established the consistency of mxPBF and derived localization rates for the proposed methods under conditions that are either weaker or comparable to those required by existing approaches.
elements.sent-2411-385: Through extensive evaluations, the proposed methods demonstrated superior performance across a broad range of scenarios, outperforming current state-of-the-art techniques.
elements.sent-2411-386: Several promising directions for future research emerge from this work.
elements.sent-2411-387: The first avenue involves enhancing the computational efficiency of the proposed methods.
elements.sent-2411-388: The primary bottleneck lies in selecting the hyperparameter $\alpha$ which controls the empirical false positive rate.
elements.sent-2411-389: This process necessitates generating a large number of datasets and calculating mxPBF for each, significantly increasing the computational cost.
elements.sent-2411-390: To address this challenge, a possible solution is to calculate all pairwise Bayes factors (PBFs) for a given $\alpha$, retaining only those variables (or pairs of variables, in the case of covariance matrices) with “relatively large” PBF values.
elements.sent-2411-391: For example, the top 10\% quantile could serve as the threshold for identifying these values.
elements.sent-2411-392: Since this approach relies on relative magnitudes of the PBFs, $\alpha$ can initially be fixed arbitrarily.
elements.sent-2411-393: Subsequently, the false positive rate-based method can be applied to this reduced dataset to fine-tune $\alpha$, significantly alleviating the computational burden.
elements.sent-2411-394: A second direction for future work is to develop adaptive change point detection methods capable of detecting both large changes in a few entries and small changes distributed across many entries.
elements.sent-2411-395: The current methods rely on mxPBF, a maximum-type Bayes factor, which may encounter difficulties in identifying change points involving numerous small-magnitude signals.
elements.sent-2411-396: Designing a Bayes factor capable of aggregating small changes across multiple components could address this limitation.
elements.sent-2411-397: By integrating such an aggregation-based approach with mxPBF, a more robust change point detection method could be developed, effectively detecting both prominent changes in a few components and subtle changes spread across many components.
elements.sent-2411-398: We would like to thank Dr. Qing Yang for providing the MATLAB code to implement their method.. .

elements.sent-2411-432: In this section, we provide empirical evidence to recommend a sufficient number of simulated datasets, $N$, for the stable selection of the hyperparameter $\hat{\alpha}$, which controls the empirical false positive rate.
elements.sent-2411-433: Since we generate simulated datasets using the sample mean and covariance, and select $\hat{\alpha}$ based on their quantiles, it is expected that generating more simulated datasets will lead to a more stable selection of $\hat{\alpha}$.
elements.sent-2411-434: However, generating too many simulated datasets would require heavy computation.
elements.sent-2411-435: Therefore, we aim to find a proper range for generating samples that not only achieves stability in selecting \(\hat{\alpha}\), but is also computationally practical.
elements.sent-2411-436: Here, we design an experiment to empirically determine the sufficient range.
elements.sent-2411-437: For simplicity, we fix \(n = 500\) and \(p = 100\), consider many signals and a dense precision or covariance matrix, and set \(\mu = 1\) and \(\psi = 8\) under the alternative hypotheses for the mean and covariance structure.
elements.sent-2411-438: Additionally, we use a window size of \(n_w = 50\) and explore a wide range for the number of simulated datasets, \(N \in \{50, 100, 200, 300, 400, 500, 700, 1000\}\).
elements.sent-2411-439: For each dataset and number of simulated datasets $N$, we repeated the selection of $\hat{\alpha}$ 100 times.
elements.sent-2411-440: For other settings, we used those described in the paper.
elements.figure-2411-019: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Plots/sufficientsample.png} \caption{The selected $\hat{\alpha}$, which controlls the empirical false positive rate at 0.05, is based on 100 replications for both the mean and covariance methods, and for each of the following hypotheses: $H_{0}$ and $H_{1,M}$, with $p = 100$.} \label{fig:sufficientsample}
elements.sent-2411-441: Figure \ref{fig:sufficientsample presents the distribution of the selected $\hat{\alpha}$, controlling the empirical false positive rate at 0.05 in each case.}
elements.sent-2411-442: With a smaller number of generations, the selected \(\hat{\alpha}\) appears unstable, exhibiting a wide range.
elements.sent-2411-443: However, once the number of generations reaches 200 or 300, it appears to stabilize.
elements.sent-2411-444: When $N\ge 300$, the range does not appear to become significantly narrower.
elements.sent-2411-445: In every scenario, a similar distribution of the selected $\hat{\alpha}$ is observed, which suggests that more than $N=300$ generations are generally sufficient.
elements.sent-2411-446: In this section, we compare the performance of the proposed methods using various window settings, including both single and multiple windows, to highlight the advantages of the multiscale approach.
elements.sent-2411-447: As explained in the paper, the proposed method requires careful selection of the window size, which can be challenging in many cases.
elements.sent-2411-448: We aim to demonstrate the impact of selecting an inappropriate window size and the effectiveness of the multiscale method.
elements.sent-2411-449: For simplicity, we fix $n=500$ and $p=200$, considering only F1 scores.
elements.sent-2411-450: We compared the performance of five proposed methods using different single windows against two versions of mxPBF\_major that utilize five and three windows, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-451: For mxPBF\_major with five windows, we use a set of window sizes \( \mathcal{N _F = \{25, 40, 60, 80, 100\} \) and denote this method as Full.}
elements.sent-2411-452: For mxPBF\_major with three windows, we use a set of window sizes \( \mathcal{N _D = \{25, 60, 100\} \) and refer to this method as Default.}
elements.sent-2411-453: For the methods using a single window, we use \( \mathcal{N =25, \mathcal{N =40, \mathcal{N =60, \mathcal{N =80, \mathcal{N =100\), respectively, denoting these methods by their window sizes.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-454: Unless indicated otherwise, we use the same settings described in the paper.
elements.subsection-2411-016: Performance of single window methods in the mean structure
elements.sent-2411-455: First, we compare the performance in detecting changes in the mean structure.

elements.sent-2411-458: Among the single-window methods, larger windows tend to detect more false positives.
elements.sent-2411-459: Both the Default and Full perform well in this regard, outperforming the single-window methods.
elements.figure-2411-021: \includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{Plots/f1_single_mean_200_h1r.png} \caption{F1 scores for change point detection in mean structure based on 50 simulated datasets under \( H_{1,R} \) with \( p = 200 \). elements.sent-2411-461: Within the single-window methods, window size 25 demonstrates inferior performance in most scenarios, indicating that it is insufficient for capturing signals.
elements.sent-2411-462: Additionally, window size 100 struggles to identify all changes in scenarios with multiple change points, as it fails to detect subsequent change points.
elements.sent-2411-463: In this regard, it can be said that window size 25 is suboptimal in most scenarios, particularly those with weak signals, while window size 100 is unsuitable for scenarios with multiple change points.
elements.sent-2411-464: Meanwhile, window sizes 40, 60, and 80 demonstrate decent overall performance, making them suitable options.
elements.sent-2411-465: Thus, Default and Full can be viewed as using one inappropriate window in single change scenarios and two in multiple change scenarios out of three and five windows, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-466: However, in most scenarios, both Full and Default demonstrate comparable performance to the appropriate window sizes.
elements.sent-2411-467: Additionally, Full slightly outperforms Default in multiple change point scenarios.
elements.figure-2411-022: \includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{Plots/f1_single_mean_200_h1m.png} \caption{F1 scores for change point detection in mean structure based on 50 simulated datasets under \( H_{1,M} \) with \( p = 200 \). elements.sent-2411-469: In this setting, window size 25 does not appear to be suboptimal for \(\mu \geq 1\).
elements.sent-2411-470: Additionally, in multiple change point scenarios, window sizes 80 and 100 struggle to identify all changes.
elements.sent-2411-471: This suggests that the optimal window size depends on both the number and magnitude of the signals.
elements.sent-2411-472: In these scenarios, Full uses one inappropriate window in single-change scenarios where $\mu < 1$, two in multiple-change scenarios where $\mu \geq 1$, and three in multiple-change scenarios where $\mu < 1$, out of five windows.

elements.sent-2411-474: However, similar to the ``rare signals" scenarios, both Full and Default show robust performance, with Full performing slightly better in multiple change point scenarios.
